question: Why does `0.1 + 0.2` not equal `0.3` in JavaScript, and how should monetary values be safely handled to avoid this issue?
answer: JavaScript uses IEEE 754 double-precision floating-point, which stores numbers in binary (base-2). Decimal fractions like 0.1 and 0.2 have infinite binary representations and are approximated when stored. Adding two approximations propagates the error, producing 0.30000000000000004 instead of 0.3. For monetary values, store amounts as integers representing the smallest currency unit (cents) or use a decimal arithmetic library like decimal.js. Never use raw floats for currency because rounding errors compound across thousands of transactions, creating significant financial discrepancies.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A["0.1 in decimal"] -->|"Binary conversion"| B["Repeating binary:
0.0001100110011..."]
  C["0.2 in decimal"] -->|"Binary conversion"| D["Repeating binary:
0.0011001100110..."]
  B -->|"52-bit truncation"| E["Approximated 0.1"]
  D -->|"52-bit truncation"| F["Approximated 0.2"]
  E -->|"Add"| G["0.30000000000000004"]
  F -->|"Add"| G
  G -->|"=== 0.3?"| H["false"]
  
  I["Store as cents × 100"] -->|"0.1 → 10, 0.2 → 20"| J["10 + 20 = 30"]
  J -->|"Integer addition"| K["30 / 100 = 0.3"]
  K -->|"=== 0.3?"| L["true"]
  
  style H fill:#f96,stroke:#333
  style L fill:#9f6,stroke:#333
